text:
kerasnip: A Bridge Between ‘keras’ and ‘tidymodels’
Madrid R Group | February 2026
kerasnip v0.1.0.900
Docs: davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip · Repo: github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip

Open with energy. Ask: “Who loves tidymodels?” then “Who fights with Keras architectures?”
Say: - Keras and tidymodels are like oil and water — kerasnip is the emulsifier. - Today: solving the architectural tuning problem.
If stuck: “Keras builds freely; tidymodels tunes systematically. We bridge them.”

Today

Why tuning Keras inside tidymodels is hard
What kerasnip generates (and why it matters)
Sequential workflow: structural tuning with num_{block}
Functional workflow: branch/merge graphs with inp_spec()
Debugging + practical tips


Goal: orient people so they don’t get lost later.
Say: - Timing is flexible; clarity first. - First half: sequential workflow + structural tuning (num_{block}) + debugging. - Second half: functional graphs via inp_spec() with the same tuning workflow.
Also mention (fast): - Debug helpers: extract_valid_grid(), inform_errors(). - Keras object helpers: keras_optimizers, keras_losses, keras_metrics, optimizer_function(), loss_function_keras(). - Spec lifecycle: model_exists(), remove_keras_spec(), get_model_env().
Transition: Let’s situate Keras vs tidymodels and name the gap.

The Landscape


🧱 Keras (via keras3)

Deep Learning
Tensor-centric & Imperative
Backend agnostic (TF, JAX, Torch)
Arbitrary graphs of layers


🧹 Tidymodels

Machine Learning
Dataframe-centric & Declarative
Unified interface (fit, predict)
Standardized tuning


The Gap: Using tune_grid() to optimize Neural Network topology (depth/width) is difficult because parsnip expects fixed arguments, while Keras models are arbitrary graphs.

Analogy: Keras = architect’s blank sheet; tidymodels = government form with fixed fields.
Say: - kerasnip turns the building’s structure into form fields. - You define the blueprint (blocks), it exposes the knobs (depth, width) as standard arguments.
If stuck: “Keras draws freely; tidymodels fills forms. kerasnip makes the forms fit the drawings.”

The Pain Point (in one sentence)
Keras models are arbitrary graphs, but parsnip specs are fixed argument sets.
So you can tune learning rate easily, but tuning model structure (depth / width / branches) is awkward.

Goal: make the problem feel concrete and familiar.
Say: - This is not “deep learning 101”. It’s: how to keep Keras inside the tidymodels workflow. - The friction is that architecture changes live in Keras code, but tidymodels tuning expects named arguments.
Transition: So the question becomes: how do we turn topology decisions into tunable spec args?

Ecosystem Context








Package
Role
Limitation




parsnip::mlp()
Tidymodels MLP
Mostly fixed topology; little architecture/feature engineering


reticulate
Python Bridge
Low-level, no ML workflow


keras3
R Interface
No native tune() integration


luz/brulee
Torch + Tidymodels
Torch-only backend


kerasnip
Keras + Tidymodels
Bridges Keras flexibility with Tidymodels workflows




Goal: position kerasnip precisely (avoid confusion).
Say: - Not replacing keras3. - Not a new engine. - It generates new parsnip model specs that still use the existing keras engine.
Also say explicitly: - parsnip::mlp() is great for standard MLPs, but limited for architecture search. - kerasnip focuses on tuning structure (depth/width/branches) without leaving tidymodels.
Transition: Now: what “dynamic specs” means in practice.

The Solution: Dynamic Specs
kerasnip is a Model Builder, not a Model Zoo.

Define Blocks: Functions returning Keras layers.
Meta-program Spec: create_keras_*_spec() generates a new parsnip model.
Tune Structure: Block arguments (and repetition count) can be tuned.


Goal: explain the “dynamic spec” idea in one breath.
Say: - This is a code-generation approach: you define blocks, then kerasnip generates a parsnip spec function. - Once it’s a parsnip spec, the rest is standard: recipes → workflows → tune_grid().
Transition: Keep the mental model in mind; we’ll come back to it on every example.

Mental Model
  





flowchart LR
  classDef step fill:#f5f7fa,stroke:#5c6b7a,color:#1b3a57,stroke-width:2px;
  classDef tune fill:#BF281B,stroke:#BF281B,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;

  A["Blocks<br/>(layer blocks)"] --> B["Spec function<br/>create_keras_*_spec()"]
  B --> C["Workflow<br/>recipe() + workflow()"]
  C --> D["Tuning<br/>tune_grid()"]
  A -. "args → tunables<br/>num_{block} repeats" .-> B

  class A,B,C step;
  class D tune;







Goal: anchor the whole talk to one repeatable mental model.
Say: - If someone gets lost: blocks → spec → workflow → tuning.
Under the hood (if asked): - Specs are registered in an internal environment. - That’s why there are lifecycle helpers: model_exists(), get_model_env(), remove_keras_spec().
Transition: Next: the minimal set of functions you’ll see in the examples.

Key Functions You’ll See

create_keras_sequential_spec()
create_keras_functional_spec()
inp_spec() (functional wiring)
compile_keras_grid() (debug/inspect generated graphs)
extract_parameter_set_dials() (what can be tuned?)


Say: - No need to memorize — just know each function’s role. - Other helpers exist (debugging, evaluation, housekeeping) and are covered in the docs.
Transition: Next: naming conventions, so the code reads naturally.

Naming Conventions (auto-generated args)

Arguments get prefixed by block name (e.g. body_units)
Repeatable blocks expose num_{block} (e.g. num_body)
Repetitions share the same hyperparameters (by design)

Example mapping:
dense_block(model, units = 128, dropout = 0.1) → body_units, body_dropout

Goal: remove naming confusion before we touch code.
Say: - Block args become spec args with prefixes (e.g. body_units). - Repetition is controlled by num_{block}. - Repeated blocks share hyperparameters by design.
Transition: With that mapping, the sequential example will look “normal”.

Sequential vs Functional (rule of thumb)

Sequential: one stream of features, simple stacks
Functional: multi-input, branch/merge, skip connections, explicit graphs


Goal: give a decision rule people can remember.
Say: - Use sequential for “a chain of layers”. - Use functional when you need an explicit graph: branches/merges, skips, multi-input.
Transition: We’ll start sequential (easier), then come back to functional.

Common Gotchas

Ensure the recipe outputs numeric matrices (dummy + normalize)
Start with a small grid, confirm structure via compile_keras_grid()
Keep tuning spaces reasonable (depth × width can explode)


Say: - Recipes must output numeric matrices (dummy + normalize). - Start small: confirm structure with compile_keras_grid() before tuning. - Keep search spaces reasonable; depth x width can explode.
If stuck: “Numeric in, small grid first, check the build.”
Transition: With those guardrails, let’s run a simple sequential example.

Workflow 1: Sequential API (Regression)
Goal: Predict diamond prices and tune depth + width.
Dataset: ggplot2::diamonds.

Goal: set up a simple, credible demo.
Say: - This is a tabular MLP on diamonds. Simple on purpose: the point is tuning depth/width, not feature engineering.
Transition: First we define blocks (just functions), then generate the spec.

Generate the parsnip spec
create_keras_sequential_spec(
  model_name = "diamond_mlp",
  layer_blocks = list(
    input = input_block,
    body = dense_block,  # <--- This block will be repeatable
    output = output_block
  ),
  mode = "regression"
)

Goal: make the “spec generation” moment land.
Say: - This creates a new parsnip spec function: diamond_mlp(). - From this point on, it behaves like any other tidymodels model: you can set engine, add to workflows, and tune args.
Transition: Next we’ll tune topology via num_body.

Structural Tuning
We treat topology as a hyperparameter.
spec <- diamond_mlp(
  num_body = tune(),       # <--- Depth (e.g., 1, 2, or 3 layers)
  body_units = tune(),     # <--- Width (e.g., 64 vs 128 units)
  body_dropout = tune(),
  fit_epochs = 20
) |>
  set_engine("keras")
The arguments num_{block} are automatically generated by kerasnip for any repeatable block.

Goal: make “depth is tunable” feel obvious.
Say: - num_body allows searching topology inside a standard tune_grid().
What to emphasize: - Depth becomes a first-class parameter. - Keep training controls mostly fixed during architecture search.
Practical tip: - Tune structure first (depth/width), then optionally tune optimizer/training hyperparameters.
Transition: Next: put it into a normal workflow.

Tuning Grid (including depth)
params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>
  update(
    num_body     = dials::num_terms(c(1, 4)),
    body_units   = dials::hidden_units(c(32, 256)),
    body_dropout = dials::dropout(c(0, 0.4))
  )

grid  <- grid_latin_hypercube(params, size = 20)
folds <- vfold_cv(diamonds_train, v = 5, strata = price)
res   <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = grid)

Goal: show this is just standard tidymodels tuning.
Say: - extract_parameter_set_dials() discovers tunables, then we define ranges and build a grid. - The key is that num_body is now a first-class tuning parameter.
Transition: Now let’s look at CV results and pick a candidate before touching the test set.

Results (Diamonds): CV — Table

Goal: pick 1–2 strong candidates (don’t read the whole table).
Say: - These are cross-validation estimates (mean + std_err). We’re selecting on CV, not the test set. - I usually look for a “stable region”: low mean RMSE and not wildly sensitive to depth/dropout.
Transition: Let’s look at the CV plot to see interactions.

Results (Diamonds): CV — Plot


Goal: see interactions (depth × width × dropout) at a glance.
Say: - If the trend flattens, deeper models aren’t buying much. - When differences are tiny relative to noise, pick simpler.
Transition: Now we do a one-time test-set confirmation.

Results (Diamonds): Test — Table




.metric


.estimate






rmse


727.2212




mae


360.1882




rsq


0.9667





Goal: one-time confirmation.
Say: - Test metrics are for final reporting; avoid using them to decide hyperparameters. - If test is worse than CV by a lot, it’s a sign of instability or leakage.
Transition: I’ll show a quick visual sanity check on test predictions.

Results (Diamonds): Test — Plot


Say: - Points near the diagonal = good predictions; systematic deviations reveal bias. - High-price diamonds are typically harder to predict — check spread there.
If stuck: “Tight diagonal = strong fit; fan-out = heteroscedasticity.”
Transition: Next: how to debug/inspect the generated architecture.

section: Debugging: “What model did we just create?”
text:
Debugging: “What model did we just create?”
# compile_keras_grid(spec, grid, x, y)


# A tibble: 1 × 4
  num_body body_units body_dropout error
     <dbl>      <dbl>        <dbl> <chr>
1        3        128          0.2 <NA> 


# A tibble: 7 × 2
  layer       class                                          
  <chr>       <chr>                                          
1 dense_150   keras.src.layers.core.dense.Dense              
2 dropout_100 keras.src.layers.regularization.dropout.Dropout
3 dense_151   keras.src.layers.core.dense.Dense              
4 dropout_101 keras.src.layers.regularization.dropout.Dropout
5 dense_152   keras.src.layers.core.dense.Dense              
6 dropout_102 keras.src.layers.regularization.dropout.Dropout
7 dense_153   keras.src.layers.core.dense.Dense              



Say: - compile_keras_grid() confirms depth/width changes are actually happening. - extract_valid_grid() keeps only successful builds; inform_errors() summarizes failures.
Transition: Next: switch to a functional (graph) example.

Workflow 2: Functional API (Branch/Merge)
Goal: Employee Attrition (Classification).
Dataset: modeldata::attrition.
We’ll build a small branch/merge graph: - one input tensor - two parallel “towers” - concat + output

Goal: motivate why functional Keras matters.
Say: - Functional is the sweet spot when the architecture is not a straight line.
Important practical choice for a live talk: keep preprocessing simple. We use ONE input produced by a standard recipe, then create two parallel branches from the same input.
Transition: inp_spec() is the wiring mechanism.

Functional Wiring with inp_spec()
inp_spec(func, inputs) explicitly maps upstream block outputs to function arguments.
That’s the key to: - multi-input models - skip connections - branch/merge architectures

Analogy: patch cables on a synthesizer. inp_spec() labels each cable.
Say: - In Keras you assign variables (x = layer(input)). In kerasnip you declare connections by name. - inp_spec(block, "name") = “plug Tower A’s output into Concat’s input”.
If stuck: “It’s just labeling which output goes into which input.”

Build the graph (spec)
create_keras_functional_spec(
  model_name = "attrition_towers",
  layer_blocks = list(
    main_input = input_block,
    tower_a = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
    tower_b = inp_spec(dense_block, "main_input"),
    joined = inp_spec(concat_block, c(input_a = "tower_a", input_b = "tower_b")),
    output = inp_spec(output_block, "joined")
  ),
  mode = "classification"
)

Goal: show functional graphs still fit a standard workflow.
Say: - This gives us a non-linear graph while still using a standard tidymodels recipe.
If your architecture is a straight line, sequential is simpler. Functional is for “graphs”: branches, merges, skips.
If asked about multi-input: - Define multiple layer_input() blocks and wire them into downstream blocks by name using inp_spec().
Transition: Next: build the workflow and tune.

Results (Attrition): CV — Table

Say: - These are cross-validation estimates (mean + std_err). Select on CV, not on test. - Look for a stable region: good AUC with low sensitivity to tower sizes.
Transition: Let’s look at the CV plot to see interactions.





tower_a_units


tower_b_units


mean


std_err






89


84


0.8025


0.0235




41


33


0.7963


0.0192




70


64


0.7926


0.0184




120


57


0.7868


0.0277




47


24


0.7866


0.0217





Say: - Look at mean ROC AUC but also std_err. - Flat heatmap = robust architecture; pick the cheaper/smaller model. - Sharp peak = sensitive; might be a local optimum. - Rule of thumb: pick the simplest within 1 std_err of the best.

Results (Attrition): CV — Plot


Goal: see whether one tower matters more than the other.
Say: - A flat heatmap means tuning isn’t very sensitive. - A sharp peak means you should narrow the search around that region.
Transition: Now: one-time test reporting.

Results (Attrition): Test — Table




.metric


.estimate






accuracy


0.8564




roc_auc


0.8080





Goal: report final test performance once.
Say: - CV chose the architecture; test is for final confirmation. - If the dataset is imbalanced, ROC AUC is often more informative than accuracy.
Transition: Show ROC curve as a visual summary.

Results (Attrition): Test — Plot


Say: - The further the curve from the diagonal, the better the discrimination. - When results are close, pick the simplest architecture within uncertainty.
Transition: Close with practical tips and failure modes.

Practical Tips (so you don’t suffer)

Start with compile_keras_grid() before tuning
Keep blocks small and testable
Prefer a few meaningful tunables over 30 tiny ones


Say: - If something breaks in a functional graph, reduce to 2-3 blocks to isolate the error. - Keep blocks small and testable; prefer a few meaningful tunables over many tiny ones.
Transition: I’ll close with trade-offs and the roadmap.

Advantages & Limitations
Advantages

Workflow Parity: Use recipes, workflows, and tune exactly like glmnet.
Reproducibility: Topology is code. Version control your architecture.
Extensibility: Wrap any Keras layer (Transformers, RNNs) into a block.

Limitations

Manual Blocking: No pre-shipped “ResNet50” blocks yet (you must define them).
Custom Losses: Integration requires some boilerplate for custom objects.


Say: - The learning curve is writing good blocks; after that, the workflow is standard. - No pre-built blocks yet (e.g. ResNet50), but any Keras layer can be wrapped.
Transition: Close with a short roadmap and invite questions.

Roadmap

📦 Templates: High-level helpers for common architectures.
🔧 Fine-tuning: Workflows for adapting pre-trained models.

Resources

📦 Package: github.com/davidrsch/kerasnip
📑 Docs: davidrsch.github.io/kerasnip


Goal: land the talk and create a clean Q&A opening.
Say: - Recap: blocks → spec → workflow → tuning. - Invite feedback and contributions. - Leave time for Q&A.
Transition: Stop sharing, take questions.
